purpose: Choose how to model business terms, concepts, ontologies, fact types, entity classes, attributes, domains, and stewardship.
required_context: known synonyms, homonyms, and contested terms
business concepts that may not appear in the data model
fact types/propositions needed for rules or analytics
relationship to entity classes, attributes, domains, tables, and columns
ask_if_missing: Do you need glossary-only governance, conceptual modeling, or implementation traceability?
assume_if_low_risk: model concept separately from term so synonyms and homonyms are not destructive
start with business concepts and fact types before table/column mapping

branches.criteria: same word means different things or different words mean the same thing
branches.recommendation: separate signifier/term from business concept and scope term usage by language, semantic community, and context

branches.criteria: rules, policies, analytics, or automated reasoning depend on relationships among concepts
branches.recommendation: model propositions and fact types explicitly, then map fact-type elements to entity classes, attributes, and relationship roles

branches.criteria: the goal is a physical catalog only
branches.recommendation: still map tables/columns to business concepts and domains; flag unmapped columns and concept gaps

branches.criteria: controlled reference data constrains many attributes
branches.recommendation: treat the code set/domain as both business data and metadata; assign ownership, lifecycle, and validation rules

failure_modes: using a word or phrase as the concept identity
hiding homonyms under one glossary row
ignoring concepts important to the business because no column stores them yet
documenting fact types as informal prose with no structural link to rules or data models
output_checks: distinguishes term, concept, symbol/context, ontology, proposition, and fact type
states how concepts map to entity classes/attributes/domains or why they do not
marks reference data/code sets that function as metadata
